sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 131, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 130 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 4.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't complete the level (board_changed: False this time, unlike before when it was True).
Let me reconsider the goal. I've been assuming the goal is to restore the initial state or get the feet to specific positions, but maybe the goal is something else.
Let me look at the body shape more carefully. The body is a black shape that looks like a person. The head is at rows 9-13, cols 24-48. The left arm is at rows 19-23, cols 14-23. The right arm is at rows 54-58, cols 39-53. The torso is at rows 24-53, cols 24-48.
Wait, but the body is a single connected black shape. Let me look at the segmentation to understand the body structure better.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the feet move in opposite horizontal directions but the same vertical direction. This means:
- UP: both feet move up
- DOWN: both feet move down
- LEFT: left feet move left, right feet move right
- RIGHT: left feet move right, right feet move left
So the feet move like a pair of scissors or a stretching mechanism. When you press LEFT, the feet spread apart horizontally. When you press RIGHT, the feet come together horizontally.
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to specific positions that require a specific sequence of moves.
Let me think about the initial state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet are at the left arm (rows 19-23, cols 14-23), and the right feet are at the right arm (rows 54-58, cols 39-53).
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the head (rows 9-13, cols 24-48). The head is a single region, so maybe both feet need to be at the head.
But the head is at rows 9-13, cols 24-48. The left feet need to move up (from rows 19-23 to rows 9-13) and right (from cols 19-23 to cols 24-48). The right feet need to move up (from rows 54-58 to rows 9-13) and possibly adjust the columns.
But the feet move in opposite horizontal directions. So if I move the left feet right, the right feet move left. This might not help.
Wait, let me reconsider. The initial state has the left feet at cols 19-23 and the right feet at cols 44-48. The head is at cols 24-48. So the left feet need to move right (from cols 19-23 to cols 24-48), and the right feet are already at cols 44-48, which is within the head's column range.
If I press RIGHT, the left feet move right and the right feet move left. So the left feet would move from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28, and the right feet would move from cols 44-48 to cols 39-43. This would bring the left feet closer to the head's column range, but the right feet would move away from the head's column range.
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the goal is not to get the feet to the head, but to get them to some other position. Let me look at the body shape and see if there are any other special positions.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me look at the initial image and see if there's a visual clue about what the goal is.
Looking at the initial image, the feet are at the "hands" of the body. Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the "feet" of the body, which would be at the bottom of the body.
But the body extends from rows 9-58, and the feet are already at rows 19-23 (left) and rows 54-58 (right). The right feet are at the bottom of the body (rows 54-58), so maybe the goal is to get the left feet to the bottom of the body as well.
But the left feet are at rows 19-23, and the bottom of the body is at rows 54-58. To move the left feet down, I need to press DOWN. But pressing DOWN also moves the right feet down, and the right feet are already at rows 54-58, which is the bottom of the body. So the right feet might be blocked from moving down.
Let me test this by pressing DOWN.
Actually wait - I previously observed that the left feet at rows 19-23 were blocked from moving down. Let me check if the left feet at rows 14-18 are also blocked from moving down.
Current state:
- Left feet: rows 14-18, cols 14-18
- Right feet: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
Let me try pressing DOWN to see if the feet can move down.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 14-18, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 49-53, cols 39-43 (right)
# Let me try pressing DOWN to see if feet can move down

action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 19-23, cols 14-18
Feet: rows 54-58, cols 39-43
